---
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss
        slide-number: true
        chalkboard: true
        title-slide: false
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor

plt.style.use('default')
fig_size_x = 8
fig_size_y = 4
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 10

def logistic_function(x, L, k, x0):
    return L / (1 + np.exp(-k * (x - x0)))

time = np.linspace(0, 20, 100)
true_population = logistic_function(time, L=500, k=0.5, x0=8)

np.random.seed(42)
observed_time = np.array([2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18])
observed_population = logistic_function(observed_time, L=500, k=0.5, x0=8) + np.random.normal(0, 50, len(observed_time))
observed_sst = 15 + 2 * np.sin(observed_time / 5) + 0.5 * observed_time + np.random.normal(0, 0.5, len(observed_time))
```

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

[EDS 232]{.custom-title}

<hr class="hr-teal">

[Lesson 2]{.custom-subtitle}

[*Learning from data*]{.custom-subtitle}

---

## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"}

[In this lesson]{.slide-title}

<hr>


<br>

- Estimating a function $f$ from observed data
- Inference vs. prediction
- Parametric vs. non-parametric methods
- Regression vs. classification
- Supervised vs. unsupervised learning

---

## {#section-learning-from-data data-menu-title="# Learning from data #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">A guiding example</p>
</div>

---

## {#researching-elephant-seals data-menu-title="Researching elephant seals"}

[Researching elephant seals]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Amanda is a marine scientist investigating how protected areas affect elephant seal populations in California. She wants to understand:
:::

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
*How does elephant seal population change*

*in a rookery that has just been protected?*
:::

---

## {#predictor-response data-menu-title="Predictor and response variables"}

[Amanda identifies the following variables]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **Predictor variable:** time since the rookery was protected
- **Response variable:** elephant seal population
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
# GRAPH 1: A simple graph that only has the axes labelled. The x axis is time and the y axis is elephant seal population.

plt.figure(figsize=( fig_size_x,  fig_size_y))
plt.xlabel('Time since rookery was protected (years)')
plt.ylabel('Elephant seal population')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 20)
plt.ylim(0, 600)
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#true-function data-menu-title="The true function f"}

[An ideal function $f$ exists somewhere...]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
What if Amanda had the *true* relationship between time and population?
:::

::: {.body-text-m}
In mathematical terms this means having a function $f$ such that
:::

$$f(\text{time since protection}) = \text{true population}$$


```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.5
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.plot(time, true_population, 'b-', linewidth=3, label='f (true relationship)')
plt.xlabel('Time since rookery was protected (years)')
plt.ylabel('Elephant seal population')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 20)
plt.ylim(0, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#checkin-1 data-menu-title="Check-in 1" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>


$$f(\text{time since protection}) = \text{true population}$$

<p class="custom-subtitle3">What information could Amanda acquire if she had this function $f$?</p>
</div>

---

## {#observed-data data-menu-title="Estimating f from data"}

[...but we have to estimate $f$ from data]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Amanda has no access to the true $f$. Her next best option:
:::

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
 **estimate $f$ using observed data**.
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.5
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.scatter(observed_time, observed_population, s=60, c='red', alpha=0.7, label='Observed data')
plt.xlabel('Time since rookery was protected (years)')
plt.ylabel('Elephant seal population')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 20)
plt.ylim(0, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#observation-error data-menu-title="Observation error"}

[Observed values $\neq$ true values]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Error is introduced during measurement:
:::

$$f(\text{time}) = \text{true population} = \text{observed population} + \epsilon$$

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.2
plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y - 0.5))
plt.plot(time, true_population, 'b-', linewidth=3, label='f (true relationship)')
plt.scatter(observed_time, observed_population, s=60, c='red', alpha=0.7, label='Observed data')
i = 0
for t, obs_pop in zip(observed_time, observed_population):
    true_pop = logistic_function(t, L=500, k=0.5, x0=8)
    if i == 0:
        plt.plot([t, t], [obs_pop, true_pop], 'g--', linewidth=1, label='Observation error')
        i += 1
    else:
        plt.plot([t, t], [obs_pop, true_pop], 'g--', linewidth=1)
plt.xlabel('Time since rookery was protected (years)')
plt.ylabel('Elephant seal population')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 20)
plt.ylim(0, 600)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#estimates-f data-menu-title="Choosing an estimate"}

[Many possible estimates for $f$]{.slide-title}

<hr>

With these observed population values at a given time, Amanda can try to

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
estimate the real function $f$ with another **function $\hat{f}$**.
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4


plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.scatter(observed_time, observed_population, s=60, c='red', alpha=0.7, label='Observed data')

linear_coeffs = np.polyfit(observed_time, observed_population, 1)
plt.plot(time, np.polyval(linear_coeffs, time), 'orange', linewidth=2, label='Linear fit')

def logistic_simple(x, L, k, x0):
    return L / (1 + np.exp(-k * (x - x0)))

popt, _ = curve_fit(logistic_simple, observed_time, observed_population, p0=[500, 0.5, 8])
plt.plot(time, logistic_simple(time, *popt), 'green', linewidth=2, label='Logistic fit')

poly_coeffs = np.polyfit(observed_time, observed_population, 8)
plt.plot(time, np.polyval(poly_coeffs, time), 'purple', linewidth=2, label='Degree 8 polynomial')

plt.xlabel('Time since rookery was protected (years)')
plt.ylabel('Elephant seal population')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.xlim(0, 20)
plt.ylim(0, 600)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m}
How do we choose? Preliminary knowledge of the data, the goal (prediction or inference?), available data volume, error — every choice involves **tradeoffs**.
:::

---

## {#section-abstract-setup data-menu-title="# Abstract setup #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">The general framework</p>
</div>

---

## {#abstract-setup data-menu-title="Abstract setup"}

[The general framework]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

We have $p$ **predictors** $X_1, \ldots, X_p$ and a **response variable** $Y$.

<br>

. . .

We assume there is a relationship:

$$Y = f(X) + \epsilon$$

where $f$ is a fixed but *unknown* function of the predictors $X_1, ..., X_p$ and $\epsilon$ is an error term (independent of $X$, mean zero).

<br>

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
Our goal: find an estimate $\hat{f}(X)$ that approximates $f(X)$ well.
:::

---

## {#why-estimate-f data-menu-title="Why estimate f?"}

[Why estimate $f$?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.teal-text .body-text-m}
**1. Inference**
:::
Use $\hat{f}$ to understand *which* predictors are associated with the response and understand the form of this relationship.

<br>

. . .

::: {.teal-text .body-text-m}
**2. Prediction**
:::
Calculate $\hat{Y} = \hat{f}(X)$ to predict the value of $Y$ for new inputs $X$.

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
The method we choose often depends on whether our goal is inference, prediction, or both. 

Interpretable models may not predict as well, and vice versa.
:::

---

## {#checkin-2 data-menu-title="Check-in 2" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What are some problems or scenarios where **inference** may be more important than prediction?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What about problems or scenarios where **prediction** is the priority?</p>
</div>

---

## {#training-data data-menu-title="Training data and notation"}

[The ML approach]{.slide-title}

<hr>

We start with a set of $n$ **observed data points**. These are called the **training set**:

$$\{(x_1, y_1),\, \ldots,\, (x_n, y_n)\}$$

<br>

- $x_i$ = a **vector** of predictor values for observation $i$: $\;\; x_i = (x_{i1}, \ldots, x_{ip})$
- $y_i$ = the **response value** for observation $i$

<br>

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
"Training" means using these data points to find an estimate $\hat{f}$.

The "learning" in *machine learning* is precisely adjusting $\hat{f}$ to minimize error on this data.
:::

---

## {#notation-example-1 data-menu-title="One variable notation example"}

[Example: One variable notation]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
With **one predictor variable** (time), Amanda's data maps to the following vectors:
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
| Obs. | Time (Predictor) |  Population (Response)|
|-------------|------|------------|
| 1 | 2 | 53 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| 5 | 10 | 353 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| 9  | 18 | 473 |
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
\

\

\

\

\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ **→**
:::

::: {.column width="35%"}
\

$(x_1,y_1) = (2,53)$

$\vdots$

$(x_5,y_5) = (10,353)$

$\vdots$

$(x_9,y_9) = (18,473)$

:::

::::

## {#notation-example-2 data-menu-title="Multiple variables notation example"}

[Example: Multi-variable notation]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
If we were to add average annual sea surface temperature (SST) as a second predictor variable, then each $x_i$ becomes a vector:
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
| Obs. | Time (Predictor 1) | SST (Predictor 2) | Population (Response)|
|-------------|------|------|------------|
| 1 | 2 | 17.05| 53 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |  $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| 5 | 10 | 20.86 | 353 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |  $\vdots$ |
| 9  | 18 | 23.27| 473 |
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
\

\

\

\

\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ **→**
:::

::: {.column width="35%"}

$x_1 = (x_{1,1}, x_{1,2}) = (2, 17.05)$  

$y_1 = 53$

$\vdots$

$x_5 = (x_{5,1}, x_{5,2}) = (10, 20.86)$  

$y_5 = 353$

$\vdots$

$x_9 = (x_{9,1}, x_{9,2}) = (18, 23.27)$ 

$y_9 = 473$

:::

::::

---

## {#section-parametric data-menu-title="# Parametric vs. non-parametric #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Parametric vs. non-parametric methods</p>
</div>

---

## {#parametric data-menu-title="Parametric methods"}

[Parametric methods]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Parametric methods estimate $f$ in two steps:

<br>

. . .

**Step 1:** Assume a *functional form* for $f$

> e.g., linear: $f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p$

. . .

**Step 2:** Use the training data to **fit** the model and estimate the parameters

> e.g., find $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p$ that best match the observations

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
[**Pros:**]{.teal-text} Works with small datasets · computationally efficient · easier inference

[**Cons:**]{.teal-text} Assumed form may be far from true $f$ · predictions may suffer
:::

---

## {#parametric-example data-menu-title="Parametric example"}

[Parametric example: linear model]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Amanda decides to model elephant seal population as **linear in time and SST**:

$$f(x_1, x_2) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\, \text{time} + \beta_2\, \text{SST}$$

Training the model finds $\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2$ that best fit the observed data. The  graph of the fitted $\hat{f}$ is a plane:

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.5
X = np.column_stack([observed_time, observed_sst])
y = observed_population
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)
beta_0 = model.intercept_
beta_1, beta_2 = model.coef_

time_range = np.linspace(observed_time.min(), observed_time.max(), 20)
sst_range = np.linspace(observed_sst.min(), observed_sst.max(), 20)
time_mesh, sst_mesh = np.meshgrid(time_range, sst_range)
population_mesh = beta_0 + beta_1 * time_mesh + beta_2 * sst_mesh

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(time_mesh, sst_mesh, population_mesh, alpha=0.5, color='orange')
ax.scatter(observed_time, observed_sst, observed_population, color='red', s=80, label='Observed data')
ax.set_xlabel('Time (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('SST (°C)')
ax.set_zlabel('Population')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.center-text .body-text-s .teal-text}
The shape of $\hat{f}$ was decided *before* seeing the data. 
:::

---

## {#nonparametric data-menu-title="Non-parametric methods"}

[Non-parametric methods]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Non-parametric methods make **no assumptions** about the functional form of $f$. They seek an estimate that fits the training data without being "too rough or wiggly".

<br>

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
[**Pros:**]{.teal-text} More flexible · can capture complex relationships · potentially better predictions

[**Cons:**]{.teal-text} Needs more data · can follow noise too closely · may be less interpretable
:::

---

## {#nonparametric-example data-menu-title="Non-parametric example"}

[Non-parametric example: K-nearest neighbors]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Instead of assuming a linear form, Amanda uses **K-nearest neighbors (KNN)**. 

For any new (time, SST) pair, the model predicts population as the average of the $K$ closest training observations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.5
X = np.column_stack([observed_time, observed_sst])
y = observed_population

time_range = np.linspace(observed_time.min(), observed_time.max(), 20)
sst_range = np.linspace(observed_sst.min(), observed_sst.max(), 20)
time_mesh, sst_mesh = np.meshgrid(time_range, sst_range)

knn = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=3)
knn.fit(X, y)
population_mesh_knn = knn.predict(
    np.column_stack([time_mesh.ravel(), sst_mesh.ravel()])
).reshape(time_mesh.shape)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(time_mesh, sst_mesh, population_mesh_knn, alpha=0.4, color='green')
ax.scatter(observed_time, observed_sst, observed_population, color='red', s=80, label='Observed data')
ax.set_xlabel('Time (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('SST (°C)')
ax.set_zlabel('Population')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.center-text .body-text-s .teal-text}
The shape of $\hat{f}$ emerges entirely from the training observations, no functional form was specified.
:::

---

## {#section-reg-class data-menu-title="# Regression vs. classification #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Regression vs. classification</p>
</div>

---

## {#reg-vs-class data-menu-title="Regression vs. classification"}

[Regression vs. classification]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

Variables can be:

::: {.body-text-m}
- **Quantitative:** continuous numerical values
- **Qualitative or categorical:** values in one of $K$ classes or categories
:::

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
[**Regression problems:**]{.teal-text} have a quantitative response variable

> *Predict elephant seal population count*

[**Classification problems:**]{.teal-text} have a qualitative response variable

> *Predict seal health status: healthy / malnourished / injured*
:::

. . .

::: {.body-text-s}
We select methods based primarily on the **type of the response variable**. Predictors can often be coded regardless of type.
:::

---

## {#classification-example data-menu-title="Classification example"}

[Classification example]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Amanda trains a classifier to predict **seal health status** from weight and length:

$$f(\text{weight, length}) = \text{health status} \in \{\text{healthy, malnourished, injured}\}$$

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.5
np.random.seed(7)
healthy    = np.random.multivariate_normal([200, 180], [[300, 50], [50, 40]], 30)
malnourish = np.random.multivariate_normal([130, 155], [[200, 40], [40, 30]], 30)
injured    = np.random.multivariate_normal([170, 165], [[250, 40], [40, 30]], 30)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
for color, label, subset in zip(['green', 'orange', 'red'],
                                  ['Healthy', 'Malnourished', 'Injured'],
                                  [healthy, malnourish, injured]):
    ax.scatter(subset[:, 0], subset[:, 1], c=color, alpha=0.6, s=40, label=label)
ax.set_xlabel('Weight (kg)')
ax.set_ylabel('Length (cm)')
ax.set_title('Training data (labeled by veterinarian)')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#section-sup-unsup data-menu-title="# Supervised vs. unsupervised #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Supervised vs. unsupervised learning</p>
</div>

---

## {#supervised data-menu-title="Supervised learning"}

[Supervised learning]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

Each observation has **predictors paired with a response variable**:

$$\text{training set} = \{(x_1, y_1),\, \ldots,\, (x_n, y_n)\},$$

where each $x_i = (x_{i1}, ..., x_{ip})$ is a vector with $p$ predictors and $y_i$ is the response to $x_i$. 

<br>

. . .

The algorithm *learns* by adjusting $\hat{f}$ to predict $y$ from $x$ as accurately as possible.

<br>


Both regression and classification are supervised learning problems.

---

## {#unsupervised data-menu-title="Unsupervised learning"}

[Unsupervised learning]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Each observation has **features only**, there is no response variable:

$$\text{training set} = \{x_1,\, \ldots,\, x_n\}$$

where each $x_i = (x_{i1}, ..., x_{ip})$ is a vector with $p$ features.

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
Example
:::

Amanda records dive depth and duration for many seals, with no labels. She asks: *are there distinct foraging strategies among these seals?*

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 3.2
np.random.seed(9)
deep_long    = np.random.multivariate_normal([550, 45], [[1500, 30], [30, 20]], 30)
shallow_freq = np.random.multivariate_normal([150, 12], [[800,  20], [20,  8]], 30)
medium       = np.random.multivariate_normal([350, 28], [[1000, 25], [25, 12]], 30)
data_dive    = np.vstack([deep_long, shallow_freq, medium])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y - 0.5))

axes[0].scatter(data_dive[:, 0], data_dive[:, 1], c='steelblue', alpha=0.6, s=30)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Dive depth (m)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Dive duration (min)')
axes[0].set_title('Observed data (no labels)')
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

for color, label, subset in zip(['royalblue', 'coral', 'mediumpurple'],
                                  ['Deep & long', 'Shallow & frequent', 'Medium'],
                                  [deep_long, shallow_freq, medium]):
    axes[1].scatter(subset[:, 0], subset[:, 1], c=color, alpha=0.6, s=30, label=label)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Dive depth (m)')
axes[1].set_title('Clusters discovered')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
axes[1].legend(fontsize=8)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#checkin-3 data-menu-title="Check-in 3" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What is the key difference between the **classification** example (health status) and the **clustering** example (foraging strategies)?</p>
</div>

---

## {#recap data-menu-title="Recap"}

[Recap]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

- We want to estimate an unknown function $f$ such that $Y = f(X) + \epsilon$

- We estimate $f$ using a **training set** of observed $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs

- **Inference** uses $\hat{f}$ to understand relationships; **prediction** uses $\hat{f}$ to forecast new $Y$

- **Parametric** methods assume a functional form; **non-parametric** methods don't

- **Regression** = quantitative response; **classification** = qualitative response

- **Supervised** = response variable present; **unsupervised** = no response variable

<br>

::: {.center-text .teal-text}
Next class: assessing model accuracy
:::
